speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 18, 1996. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. My name is Esther Finder. We're in Beltsville, Maryland, and the language will be English. Today is December 18, 1996. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. My name is Esther Finder. We are in Beltsville, Maryland, and the language will be English. I'd like to thank you for doing the interview with us today. Can you tell me, what is your name, and would you spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is William, W-I-LL-I-A-M, middle name, R, middle initial, R. Last name is Perl, P-E-R-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at birth my name was Willy, W-I-L-L-Y, Perl, P-E-R-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 21st of September, 1906.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 90 years, plus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, Czechoslovakia. Then, it was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, capital of the Bohemian province of Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you feeling today, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel all right. However, I have-- I'm suffering from Parkinson's disease, for many years. And for the interview, the viewers have to be aware that I am performing with the need to present a healthy picture of a healthy person, which I am not. I make very, very many involuntary movements. They're all part of my Parkinson's disease symptoms. And I apologize for them, and ask you to concentrate on the content, and not on the weird movements which you will see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Prague until to my fourth year of age. Then my family moved to Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your family move? Why did your family move?

speaker: interviewee
text: They moved because they saw a better future for themselves. Vienna was the capital of the country and a very admired city, all over the world, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, first, a traveling salesman for a large textile firm. And then he established himself as a dealer on wholesale in textiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rudolf, R-U-D-O-L-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her name was Camilla, C-A-M-I-L-L-A. And her last, maiden name, her maiden name was Fischer, F-I-S-C-H-E-R. She was a very good-looking woman, a very warm woman, ready to give her last shirts for the poor, and very un-political.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother, Walter, W-A-L-T-E-R, with the middle name Vladimir. Prague was a bilingual city. Many people gave their names-- a German and a Slav name, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your brother older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was four years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religiously observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were religious but not in any way orthodox. We celebrated, clearly, the [INAUDIBLE], the Easter festival, the Hanukkah, and the new year, Rosh Hashanah, and Yom Kippur. On all these occasions, we went to the synagogue. My mother went every Friday evening, to the services. And she belonged to the only Jewish community with her sisters, which is in a way orthodox. There was no choir, and the men and women were not sitting together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a really Jewish area, a mixed area. I lived in an upper-class, middle-class area in Prague, and an upper-middle-class area in Vienna, where many Jews lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any religious training? Did you go to Hebrew school, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had religious training in school. At that time, it was obligatory to teach an hour, each week, of religion. And I went to the religious, to the Jewish instructions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you study in the public school, besides the Jewish religious instruction? What other subjects were interesting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends what you learned in public school. I started out with reading and writing, and in two alphabets-- in the German alphabet, and then in the Latin alphabet, which we used to [INAUDIBLE] want us into Gothic alphabet. And history-- very simple history, at that time-- very concentrated on Austria, and on the Emperor-- how big and important he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, did you belong to any Jewish social organizations, or even some political organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- yes. I first of all, I belonged to the sports club Hakoah, which was, in Vienna, very much of a center for Viennese public living. And I was a member there and attended all the football matches. They were called football, but they were actually soccer. And in the gymnasium, which is the school of eight years training between the elementary school and the university, I was a member of a Jewish fraternity-- Emuna, which means the truth. And then in the university, I was member of the Jewish academic [INAUDIBLE], which was a very active Jewish school and fraternity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study at the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied, at the University of Vienna, I studied law, with a very heavy mixing with criminology and psychology. At the-- at the same time while I was studying full time at the University of Vienna law, I was an enrolled as a full-time student at the College of Economics in Vienna, the [INAUDIBLE]. And I completed both studies in the earliest possible time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what it was like in the Jewish intellectual community, in those days? Those were the days of Freud and many other well-known Jewish intellectuals in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the intellectuals were not Zionists. They were assimilated. I did not have very much contact with them. I knew Freud, personally, because he knew my father. They came from Czechoslovakia, both, and emigrated to Austria. And I knew him, I remember him. Also, I heard him speak, once, at a meeting of the B'nai B'rith. And I met him the last time-- several times, in between-- but the last time, when the Nazis were already near to come, at the post, at the letterbox, outside the cafe where my fraternity used to assemble. And there, he had-- it was winter, very cold. And he had dropped letters into the letter box, which some of them had fallen to the ground. And I picked them up and gave them to him. And he said, thank you. And then he saw who I was, and he said, thank you, Mr. Perl. It was my last contact with Freud.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mention that most of these Jewish intellectuals were not Zionists.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very actively Zionist, at this point in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you got involved with the Zionist movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Hakoah. The Hakoah was a Zionist organization, Jewish national organization. And this was in strong contrast-- in school, already, there were supporters of the Hakoah. And there were Jewish supporters which were supporters of other organizations, which, of course, strengthened our identity as being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, and when you were in university, did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I certainly did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share some of those experiences with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very vivid experiences. When I was a little boy, maybe seven or eight years old, and I was on the playground, and a group of children played there. And I wanted to play with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they asked me, are you a Jew? And I said, yes. They said, you can't play with us. I said, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, because you killed Christ. I says, I didn't kill anybody. They said, so your father killed Christ. And I was excluded by this. And that was what I remember, vividly. But the fact of anti-Semitism was in the air, differently than here. It was more social anti-Semitism. Not social anti-Semitism, it was official anti-Semitism. And the officers and so Jews couldn't get certain jobs, and so on. But you had-- Christian friends were invited to their house, and Christians came to your house, something which is quite unusual, in America. But still, there was anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism at the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. There were regularly-- at regular intervals-- no, at irregular intervals, there were clashes between the German Nationalist students, who tried to throw the Jews out, physically, from the university. And [INAUDIBLE] developed at that time. And then they shouted, [GERMAN] out with the Jews and sang certain German patriotic songs. Well it was, at the university, it was very clearly anti-Semitic. But interesting, I was often asked whether I noticed anti-Semitism among the professors. I never did. They didn't show it. They were objective, as far as I could judge-- in the gymnasium, as well as in the university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you following the news from Germany, when Hitler came to power and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, with very much interest and concern.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from Hitler's early days in power?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember before he came to power, the putsch which he tried in Munich. But he was in jail, there. And then I was [GERMAN] assistant to a lawyer-- more than assistant, an apprentice-- who [INAUDIBLE] practically all the lawyers were too, in Salzburg, from the summer of 1931 to the summer of 1932. And there I was, in Salzburg, where there-- this was about the [? framework ?]. There were elections in Germany. And Hitler came to give his speech at Rosenheim, which is a city in southern Bavaria, not far from Salzburg. And I went to this, and that's where I saw Hitler speak. And of course, I followed all the time, the rise after this-- the direction in which he won very many votes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like for you, to be listening to Hitler speaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzburg?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzburg the anti-Semitism was much stronger. It's still there. But it's kept down to a minimum, during the Festspiele, when the tourists are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your personal impression of Hitler, when you watched him speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you when you met your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife under very odd circumstances. I was-- usually I went to bed very late. And once a week, I had what I call [GERMAN], sleeping [INAUDIBLE]. And I was going to bed early, around 7 o'clock or so. And there was still, outside, light for a long time. It was summer time. The watches were changed. And when it started to get dark, I had-- hadn't fallen asleep. I decided to go to the park for a walk. And I saw there, sitting on a chair, of a long row of empty chairs, my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at her and decided I would try to speak to her, and passed her, and came back. And to get a response at all, I asked her the stupidest question-- excuse me, is this seat free?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, idiot. All these seats are free. With this, she had responded. And we came into talking, and then I accompanied her to the entrance of her house. And then we had [INAUDIBLE] and-- there was some difficulty with-- with her, because she was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: On--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer-- in June of 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: How difficult was it for the two of you to keep company, in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not difficult at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her parents have any objection to you seeing her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you decide to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- first we decided to break up. Because I knew she's not a girl with who-- I was very much in love. And I decided, she is not a girl with whom you can try to start an affair, and then you end it. This is a girl with whom you either marry, or you don't. And so I broke up-- we broke up. That was in the fall of 1937. And at Christmas, it became unbearable for me. And I sent her-- probably unconsciously, I knew where it will end-- a big flower bush, of-- of lilac, or a big bouquet. I don't remember, anymore. And we then met and became much closer associated, and decided, then, to marry. She decided to convert. There was no objection from the fact of being a Catholic, although she had been brought up in elementary school, in a Catholic schooling of a monastery school. There was no objection from a Catholic point. But there was that she didn't actually believe-- she was not interested in Jewish religion, particularly. But she converted. And we became, then, married, under very difficult circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to get interested in the possibility of getting Jews out of Europe and into Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1936 [? this ?,] after I saw what was going on in Germany, and after German Jews had fled to Vienna. And I wasn't only concerned with them. I was also concerned with the people who came-- chalutzim-- intended to emigrate to Israel, who came on their way to Palestine through Vienna, usually without any means. And this was a reason. But the reason was also to try to get as many Jews as possible to Vienna. It was not only the humanitarian element that caused me to act. And in 1936, a man came to my office. I was a young lawyer, then-- an apprentice lawyer, but for all practical purposes, you were a lawyer. For six years, you had to work, supposedly, as an apprentice, before you could work under your own name. And a man came to my office with a letter from Dr. Wolfgang von Weisl, who was a leader, internationally-recognized leader, of Zionism. And he said, this man has an interesting suggestion, listen to him. And I listened to him. And at that time, part of Northern Africa was in Italian hands. It was an Italian colony, Libya and-- and-- I think Libya, and I don't know how the other part of North Africa was called at that time. And he intended-- Mussolini intended to make it a big show and hold a big massive fair, to the-- to-- to-- to the-- to be the massive fair of Libya. And the suggestion was-- by this man-- he was a Palestinian who has come from Russia. And a very interesting type. He was a very committed Jew, and also a real adventurer. And his suggestion was that we rent, that we take a boat-- rent a boat-- and say that we are going to the Fiera di Tripoli. The Fiera di Tripoli is what it was called. Tripoli [INAUDIBLE] and Libya were two provinces, then, of Italian rule. But we go to the Fiera di Tripoli, put Betarim on it, members of the active Jewish movement-- Jewish movement in which I have been active. And that they, on their way, with force, with weapons, force the captain to change course, and to go to Palestine. And the crew would be, actually, in part of the games, they'll be not ready.

words.text: to-- to-- to the-- to be

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, they will land. And it will be a big public relations success against Britain. Because everyone will ask, how bad must it be, if Jewish youngsters-- many of them from good families-- commit piracy? So I was interested in the subject. But I was more interested in the possibility of exploiting it for a longer time. I said, let's not-- let's do it, and let's bring them, really, there, without secrecy. And when they catch us, then we will blow the trumpet. And he agreed to that. And we had the first transport of 16 people to Palestine. There were to the best of my recollection, no Viennese youngsters among them, but only people who had come from the east, and were without any means in Vienna. And nobody believes them, and nobody knew [INAUDIBLE]. And I was a lawyer, what do I have to do with smuggling people?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they left. And within a few days, letters arrived from Palestine, postcards that said, arrived safely. [INAUDIBLE] had smuggled them into Palestine. And then there were two more transports before the Nazis came to power. I don't know, now, the exact numbers. But there were between 70 and 80 people in those transports, each. And they all worked well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a few questions about the transport. First, you mentioned a Betarim. So the particular Zionist group that was involved was the Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Betar was a Zionist youth group, part of the revisionist organisation, which, in turn, was headed by Vladimir Jabotinsky, Ze'ev Jabotinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you put together a ship of people, how did your choose who was going? There must have been any number of Betarim available. How did you make the choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it was hard to get people, because they were scared. They did not know. And for the second and third transport, I traveled to [INAUDIBLE]. That was the Russian-speaking part of Czechoslovakia, it's now the Republic of Moldavia-- to get young people from there. And we got people from there, and we got people from Slovakia, and a good number of Viennese Betarim too. But the Viennese were-- [INAUDIBLE] I remember-- in the minority.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you always concentrated on young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: On young people, yes. Old people were not ready to take the chance. And then, the young people were enterprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: On that first ship that went, were--

speaker: interviewee
text: With 16 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were there any provisions for them in Palestine? Was there anybody there to receive them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing was prepared. They went-- they went by train to Athens. And in Athens there were these smugglers, prepared a little ship, which brought them as close to the coast as possible, so that they could swim there. And they were-- it was a flat ship, flat boat. And so they swam and waded to the land. And there, they were soon taken up by the people-- by Jewish inhabitants of what was Palestine, which was, for us, always [INAUDIBLE], the Land of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you start to put together a transport like this, what are some of the first steps that you have to take? How do you go about organizing something like this? How do you make all these puzzle pieces fit together?

speaker: interviewee
text: The landing part was all [INAUDIBLE], job. I had to organize the part in Vienna. And if I say I, I have to stress that it's not I. This was a committee of people. I was a member of a small group, which was called [INAUDIBLE], which means in English, in spite of everything, we will bring them to Palestine. And the leader of this group was a journalist with the name of Paul Haller, H-A-L-L-E-R-- politically, a genius. I'm sorry that he didn't survive. He went to Israel, but he died very early, there. He was the main, driving force in it. I was not-- I was one of three or four, until '40s, we were very few. I was very active. But I was not the leader of the group. I was the leader by the name that I-- I was the only name, to which people could refer. I was a lawyer, so I presented something. Other people there at that time, [INAUDIBLE]. I don't recall, now. We had five or six people. Yeah, Eric Wolfe, Eric Wolfe was not a member of the group. But he was in charge [INAUDIBLE] he was an [INAUDIBLE] of the Betar, with whom we cooperated. And he organized everything in the Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now, to change tape. And when we come back, I'll ask you more about organize--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'll walk a little bit.
